preFix.title: Spinning a gradient singles from a hand-dyed batt
preFix.subtitle: Peeling strips across the batt width to preserve colour order through one continuous draw
preFix.excerpt: A hand-dyed gradient batt holds its colour sequence in layers across the width of the drum. Peel strips across that width rather than lengthways and you spin through every colour change in order, producing a gently shifting gradient singles. Ply from both ends of the same centre-pull ball and the two colour sequences meet in the middle, creating a barber-pole 2-ply with long colour repeats.
preFix.sourceNotes: The cross-width strip method for gradient batts is a standard technique taught in fibre arts workshops and documented by gradient dyers supplying the handspinner market. The barber-pole plying approach from a centre-pull ball is widely used for gradient colour effects.

attrs.body: Keep loose hair and clothing back from the drive band and flyer. The flyer turns fast; tie hair back and roll sleeves.
attrs.title: Wheel safety

content.text: A hand-dyed gradient
batt

holds its colour sequence across the width of the drum: if the dyer started with yellow at one edge and moved through orange to red at the other, those colours are stacked in layers from left to right across the batt sheet. The mistake most spinners make is to tear or roll strips lengthways along the batt, which mixes the colours into a tweedy effect. To preserve the gradient, the strips must be peeled across the width.
Once you have a continuous gradient
singles
on a bobbin, wind it into a centre-pull ball.
Plying

from both ends of that ball creates a 2-ply. The outside end holds the starting colour. The inside end holds the finishing colour. The two colour sequences run in opposite directions, creating a long-repeat barber-pole stripe.
What you need

items.name: Spinning wheel (castle or Saxony), set to a mid-range ratio (7:1 to 9:1)
Hand carders (optional, to tidy strip joins)
Ball winder or nostepinne for a centre-pull ball

content.text: Step 1: Read the batt

Lay the batt flat on a table with the colour sequence running left to right. Note which edge holds the starting colour (colour A) and which holds the finishing colour (colour B). Keep this orientation as you peel strips.
Step 2: Peel strips across the width

Starting from the colour-A edge (left), gently peel a strip from the batt. The strip should run from colour A all the way across to colour B: this is the full colour range in one strip. Work across from left to right, keeping the strip about 3-4 cm wide for a DK-weight singles. Lay the peeled strips in sequence beside the wheel, keeping their left-to-right orientation.
Do not fold the strips or mix their order. If the batt has a very dense surface and the strips tear rather than peel cleanly, use hand carders to lightly loosen the surface layer before peeling.
Step 3: Spin the gradient

Spin from the colour-A end of the first strip. Use a long draw suited to the batt structure: the fibres are not aligned and a short forward draw will not give the same loft. As each strip ends, join the next strip at the same colour point: the end of strip one (colour B) joins to the start of strip two (also colour A) to continue the gradient cycle.
Spin all strips onto one bobbin without removing the yarn between strips. This single continuous singles holds the full colour sequence from one end to the other.
Step 4: Wind a centre-pull ball

Wind the completed singles from the bobbin into a centre-pull ball on a ball winder or by hand on a nostepinne. Keep track of which end is the start (colour A) and which is the finish (colour B). Mark one end with a small loose knot if needed.
Step 5: Ply from both ends

Thread both the outside end and the centre end of the ball through the orifice and ply S-twist at a ratio of approximately 5:1 to 6:1. The two colour sequences will be out of phase with each other throughout the entire skein, producing the barber-pole effect. Ply at an even speed and check the strand balance every few metres: let a short loop hang free; it should hang with only a slight curl.
Common problems

items.fix: Check strip orientation before spinning the next batt. A quick test: hold a peeled strip up to light and check whether the two ends show clearly different colours. If they look the same, the strip is cut lengthways.
items.cause: Strips were peeled lengthways along the batt rather than across the width, mixing the colour layers.
items.problem: The gradient is muddy rather than clearly graduated

items.fix: Re-wind more firmly. Place the ball in a smooth bowl so it stays still while both ends are drawn from it. If the ball collapses mid-ply, stop, unwind the tangled section, and continue.
items.cause: The ball was wound too loosely and the layers are not staying separated.
items.problem: The centre-pull ball tangles when both ends are drawn out during plying

items.fix: At each join, split both the ending strip and the starting strip lengthways and overlap only the thin halves. The splice will be no thicker than a single strip.
items.cause: The join point was overlapped rather than thinned, adding extra fibre at the splice.
items.problem: The strip joins are visible as thick sections in the finished yarn

initialFindings.message: TECHNIQUE tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
